channel.title: KillBait - News highlights delivered clearly and responsibly—no clickbait, no sensationalism.
channel.description: KillBait is a news aggregator developed by students and professionals in journalism and web development from Argentina, Spain, and the United Kingdom. Our focus is on providing readers with clear, concise, and relevant summaries of each news story.

item.title: What is KillBait?
item.description: Surely you've clicked on a news article with an eye-catching or sensational headline...

item.title: How KillBait works

item.title: Upcoming news
Jessica Sutta Suggests Political Views Led to Exclusion from Pussycat Dolls Reunion
item.description:
Jessica Sutta, a former member of the Pussycat Dolls, revealed in a recent interview that she believes her conservative and Republican political stance may have played a role in her exclusion from the group’s upcoming reunion. Speaking on the March 22 episode of The Mavericks Approach podcast, Sutta described herself as a 'liability' due to her alignment with Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the current U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services. She explained that her support for Kennedy, whom she campaigned for during his 2024 presidential run, stems from her personal experience with what she describes as a vaccine-related neurological condition. Sutta clarified that while she does not fully support former President Donald Trump, she identifies with aspects of the MAGA movement because of Kennedy’s health policies and advocacy for vaccine-injured individuals.
Sutta shared that she and other former members—Carmit Bachar and Melody Thornton—were not contacted about the reunion, stating they w

item.title: Progress MS-33 Successfully Launched and Docked with ISS Using Manual TORU System
item.description: On March 24, 2026, the Progress MS-33 cargo spacecraft was launched atop a Soyuz 2.1a rocket from Baikonur Cosmodrome. Carrying 2509 kg of cargo, including food, water, oxygen, and fuel, the Progress MS-33 is essential for maintaining the ISS. This mission marked the reopening of the PU-6 launch pad, which had been damaged during a previous Soyuz launch in November. The Progress MS-33 successfully docked with the Russian segment of the ISS, but with a notable challenge: the Kurs radar system, typically used for automatic docking, failed to deploy. As a result, the spacecraft had to be manually docked using the TORU system, a teleoperated procedure. While the TORU system has had a history of successful operations, it is often associated with past mishaps. The manual docking was successfully carried out by cosmonaut Sergey Kud-Sverchkov from the ISS, showcasing the versatility of the Russian space program even in challenging circumstances.

item.title: The Centuries-Long Sunni-Shiite Divide: A Struggle for Power Rooted in the Death of Prophet Muhammad
item.description: The schism between Sunnis and Shiites traces back over fourteen centuries to the death of Prophet Muhammad in 632 CE. The absence of a clear successor sparked a deep division, with one group advocating for leadership based on consensus (Sunni) and the other believing the Prophet designated his cousin and son-in-law, Ali, as his successor (Shiite). This initial power struggle ignited a series of violent confrontations, from the murders of the first caliphs to the brutal massacre at Karbala in 680, where Husayn, the grandson of the Prophet, was killed. Despite centuries of conflict, the division remains unresolved, with both groups using religion as a foundation for political power. In modern times, the rivalry continues to shape geopolitics, particularly between Iran (a Shiite-majority country) and Sunni powers like Saudi Arabia. This ongoing conflict is fueled not only by religious ideology but also by territorial, ethnic, and political interests. The historical and ongoing struggle be

item.title: Why U.S. Protests Miss the Point: The Empire, Not Trump, Is the Problem
item.description: Caitlin Johnstone argues that U.S. protests, like the 'No Kings' demonstrations, miss the deeper issue of the U.S. empire. She critiques the way these protests focus on individual leaders, such as Donald Trump, without challenging the systemic violence and imperialism embedded in the U.S. political structure. Johnstone highlights the constant, unpunished violence committed by U.S. presidents and the broader imperial agenda that fuels conflicts, especially in places like Iran. The protests, she argues, are shallow and avoid addressing the root cause of U.S. oppression, instead calling for a more polished and humane version of the empire. She stresses that the true change needed is revolutionary, rather than symbolic protests against individual figures who merely represent the ongoing status quo. This piece critiques the American liberal establishment for focusing on surface-level issues while ignoring the destructive forces of imperialism that shape global politics.

item.title: Trump's $200 Billion Request Signals Major Escalation in Iran Conflict
item.description: The Trump administration has requested an additional $200 billion for its military operations against Iran, a move that underscores the scale of the U.S.'s growing involvement in the Middle East. This amount is separate from the already approved $839 billion defense budget for fiscal year 2026, bringing the total military spending for this year to more than $1 trillion. The proposal reflects a significant escalation of the U.S. military’s presence, including a possible ground invasion of Iran. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth confirmed the figure and hinted that it could increase further. The funds are aimed at seizing key Iranian assets such as Kharg Island, a major oil export hub, and securing Iran’s uranium stockpiles. Critics warn that this is part of a long-term strategy to engage in open-ended wars, potentially involving other nations like China, which the U.S. sees as a global threat. The $200 billion request is framed as a necessary cost for national security but also raises conc

item.title: House Committee Turns Over Classified Records to Prosecutors in Brennan Investigation Ahead of Charging Decision
item.description:
Federal prosecutors are set to receive classified documents from the House Intelligence Committee as part of the ongoing Justice Department investigation into former CIA Director John Brennan. The committee voted in a closed session to grant access to the records, including a transcript of an earlier interview with Brennan. The move signals that the Justice Department’s inquiry may be approaching a decision on whether to file charges against him.
The probe focuses on allegations that Brennan misled Congress about an intelligence assessment regarding Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election. Although Brennan has consistently denied wrongdoing, prosecutors in Miami and Washington, D.C. have been examining evidence for several months. Some Justice Department officials have questioned the strength of the case, yet prosecutors continue to move forward with key procedural steps required before an indictment can be considered.
Earlier this month, prosecutors issued a subpoena

item.title: Volkswagen Considers Converting Osnabrück Plant to Produce Iron Dome Components
item.description: Volkswagen is reportedly in discussions with Israel's Rafael Advanced Defence Systems to transform its Osnabrück factory from a car manufacturing plant into a facility that produces components for the Iron Dome missile defense system. The German government is supporting this initiative, and production could begin within 12 to 18 months. Historically, Volkswagen was founded during the Third Reich and utilized forced labor from Jewish and non-Jewish workers across Eastern Europe, including operating concentration and forced-labor camps on its premises. Today, the company is pivoting from civilian vehicle manufacturing to defense-related production, marking a significant strategic shift. This move has sparked public debate and discussions online, reflecting both historical sensitivities and contemporary geopolitical considerations. The report highlights the contrast between Volkswagen’s controversial origins and its modern involvement in military production for Israel.

item.title: Lynx Photograph Wins Wildlife Photographer of the Year People's Choice Award
item.description: The winner of the 2026 Wildlife Photographer of the Year People's Choice award has been announced, with over 60,000 entries and public voting determining the top image. The winning photo, chosen from 24 shortlisted images, features a young lynx in Ciudad Real, Spain, tossing a rodent into the air before killing and eating it. This image highlights the success of conservation efforts that have brought the Iberian lynx back from near extinction, with the species now numbering over 2,000. The award showcases a variety of stunning wildlife photos, including polar bears, flamingos, bear cubs, and a sika deer. Four other photos were highly commended in the public vote, including a striking image of flamingos set against industrial backdrops in Namibia, and a dramatic photo of a mother polar bear with her cubs in Canada. The winner and the top images will be displayed at the Wildlife Photographer of the Year exhibition in London, open until 12 July. This year's competition shines a light on b

item.title: Jay-Z opens up about anger and legal battles following dropped sexual assault lawsuit
item.description: Jay-Z has spoken out for the first time about the sexual assault allegations made against him and Sean 'Diddy' Combs in 2024. The rapper revealed that he experienced 'uncontrollable anger' after an anonymous accuser filed a lawsuit, accusing both men of an incident at a party in 2000. Jay-Z and Combs both denied the claims, calling them a 'blackmail attempt' and 'heinous in nature'. The case was eventually dropped in February 2025. During an interview with GQ to mark the 30th anniversary of his debut album, *Reasonable Doubt*, Jay-Z explained the emotional toll the situation took on him and his family. He mentioned the trauma endured by himself, his wife Beyoncé, their children, and loved ones, describing the fictional narrative created by the accuser as 'laughable' but serious in its implications. The rapper also pursued legal action, filing a motion to force the accuser to reveal their identity and suing the accuser and their lawyer for conspiring to defame him and cause financial da

item.title: Evolving Leadership for a Rapidly Changing, AI-Driven World
item.description: In 2026, leadership is transforming rapidly due to technological advances and changing workforce expectations. Companies are moving away from traditional top-down management structures, emphasizing human-centric leadership where employee well-being, empowerment, and psychological safety are prioritized. Burnout has become a critical issue, making empathetic, emotionally intelligent leaders highly valuable. Alongside this, AI fluency has become essential; leaders are expected to collaborate effectively with AI systems for decision-making, operational efficiency, and ethical management. Human-machine partnerships are enabling leaders to enhance their empathy and improve consistency in tasks such as customer service and HR management. Adaptive and agile leadership is also key in responding to global uncertainties, technological disruptions, and economic volatility. Organizations that encourage experimentation, scenario planning, and continual upskilling are best positioned to succeed. Lea

item.title: Iran’s Conditions for a Cease-Fire in Ongoing Middle East Conflict
item.description:
According to The Wall Street Journal’s live coverage, Iran has presented a firm and ambitious list of demands in ongoing cease-fire negotiations related to the escalating Middle East conflict involving the United States, Israel, and regional partners. Tehran’s delegation has reportedly insisted that any cease-fire must be comprehensive and accompanied by guarantees of Israeli military withdrawal from parts of Gaza and Lebanon. Iran is also seeking assurances that Washington will halt direct support for Israeli air operations and that humanitarian aid routes into Gaza will remain permanently open under international supervision.
Diplomatic sources suggest the talks remain tense as Iran’s proposals aim not only to de-escalate immediate hostilities but also to reshape long-term regional power dynamics. Western officials consider some of Iran’s asks unrealistic, especially those demanding the lifting of certain economic sanctions and the recognition of Iranian-backed militias’ political r

item.title: Bannon Proposes ICE Deployment at Airports as Trial for 2026 Midterms
item.description: Steve Bannon, former White House strategist, has raised concerns about the role of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in future elections. Speaking on his podcast 'War Room,' Bannon suggested that ICE's recent presence at airports could serve as a 'test run' for their involvement in the upcoming 2026 midterm elections. Bannon's controversial remarks highlight his ongoing push for increased immigration enforcement during elections, despite previous backlash. His guest, Mike Davis, agreed with the suggestion, proposing that ICE agents be stationed at polling locations to prevent illegal voting. The conversation centered around fears that undocumented immigrants might influence election results, a claim often made without substantial evidence. Bannon previously made similar statements regarding ICE’s role at polling stations in February, promising to ensure election integrity by preventing voter fraud. These remarks reflect Bannon's broader agenda of controlling immigration an

item.title: Crispin Odey Defends Himself in Tribunal Over Misconduct Allegations in UK Finance Ban Case
item.description: Former hedge fund manager Crispin Odey, 67, is contesting his ban from the UK financial sector in a tribunal after being accused of inappropriate conduct on at least 46 occasions between 2003 and 2020. Odey, once one of Britain’s wealthiest fund managers, was prohibited from working in finance and fined £1.8 million in 2025 for lacking integrity following numerous misconduct allegations. During his testimony at the Upper Tribunal in London, Odey denied the claims and attempted to justify one alleged incident of groping a colleague in 2005, stating it occurred because he was still under the effects of a general anesthetic after a dental procedure. Counsel for the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), Clare Sibson KC, detailed multiple allegations, including Odey’s comments and behavior toward receptionists at his firm, Odey Asset Management. Odey admitted his actions were inappropriate for his age, acknowledging that what once seemed harmless banter as a younger man had become improper and

item.title: NASA Confirms Lunar Base Plans and Cancels Gateway Station
item.description: NASA has officially outlined its updated lunar exploration plans, revealing the construction of a lunar base and the cancellation of the Lunar Gateway station. Initially planned as a non-crewed installation, the lunar base will evolve in stages, eventually supporting crewed missions. The Gateway station, long delayed and affected by international and political complications, will no longer be part of Artemis missions. Its propulsion module will be repurposed for a nuclear mission to Mars, while other modules from international partners, including ESA, JAXA, and the UAE, remain in limbo. The lunar base project will follow a phased approach: the first phase (2027-2029) will involve unmanned missions, rovers like VIPER, orbiters, and innovative hopping drones called MoonFall to explore the lunar surface, particularly shadowed craters. The second phase (2029-2033) will deploy heavier equipment, RTG-powered systems for night survival, vertical solar towers, and communication stations. The t

item.title: North Carolina State Senate Leader Defeated Despite Trump Endorsement
item.description: In North Carolina, state Senate leader Phil Berger, who had been endorsed by former President Donald Trump, unexpectedly lost his primary election to conservative challenger Steve Jarvis. The result was striking considering Berger’s long influence in North Carolina Republican politics, where he played a central role in shaping major policy decisions over the last decade, including education reform and tax policy. Trump's endorsement was expected to solidify Berger’s position, but local factors appeared more decisive. Jarvis, a state representative known for his appeal to grassroots conservatives, campaigned on issues like border security and government accountability, presenting himself as an alternative to entrenched leadership. The race reflected growing fractures within the Republican Party in the state, with some voters leaning toward candidates who promise tighter alignment with Trump’s populist style rather than long-serving party figures seen as part of the establishment. Analys

item.title: New Mexico Jury Orders Meta to Pay $375 Million for Violating Consumer Protection Laws Related to Child Safety
item.description: A New Mexico jury has ruled that Meta Platforms violated state consumer protection laws by misleading users about the safety of Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, and by enabling child sexual exploitation on its platforms. The jury awarded $375 million in civil penalties to the state. The lawsuit, initiated by New Mexico Attorney General Raúl Torrez, stems from an undercover operation in 2023 where investigators created accounts posing as children under 14, exposing them to sexually explicit material and contacts from adults seeking such content. Meta denied the allegations, claiming that it implements extensive safeguards to protect young users and that the harm cited by the state cannot be separated from user-generated content, invoking First Amendment protections and Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. The trial highlighted broader concerns about the impact of Meta's platforms on youth mental health, addictive design features, and company transparency regarding risks to m

item.title: How to Fairly Divide Assets After a Creative Partnership Ends
item.description: When a creative partnership dissolves, the division of assets can become complex. The first step is to identify what counts as a shared asset—this includes physical property like equipment and digital assets like software licenses and social media accounts. Tangible items like cameras or studio equipment may require valuation based on market rates, while intellectual property like patents and copyrights can further complicate the process. Artists should document everything, including receipts for purchases and details about trademarks or active subscriptions. A fair division requires balancing empathy with professionalism, and often involves negotiation or mediation to avoid lengthy court battles. If both partners can't reach an agreement, judicial proceedings may be necessary to determine the division of assets, considering both financial and non-financial contributions. Once the assets are divided, both individuals should establish separate financial identities, update insurance poli

item.title: The Glasshouse Theatre in Brisbane: A Stunning Design Featuring Rippling Glass Facade
item.description: In Brisbane, Australia, the newly unveiled Glasshouse Theatre at the Queensland Performing Arts Centre (QPAC) is drawing attention for its stunning, undulating glass façade. Designed by Snøhetta in collaboration with Blight Rayner Architecture, this 1,500-seat theatre is covered in curved glass panels that create a dynamic, ever-changing effect. The surface reflects the surrounding landscape, including the nearby Brisbane River, creating a look that feels alive throughout the day. The glass walls not only manage glare and heat but also allow a glimpse of the theatre's activities from the outside. This transparent design enhances the connection between the building and the city, making it an inviting and modern addition to the cultural hub. The theatre, which expands the QPAC, offers a new venue that balances functionality with aesthetics. The careful engineering of the glass surface ensures that it remains both visually striking and energy-efficient. The building’s wave-like form blend

item.title: Adolescence Leads BAFTA TV Nominations with 11 Nods, Including Multiple Acting Mentions
item.description: The Netflix drama 'Adolescence' has taken the lead at the 2026 BAFTA TV Awards with 11 nominations, including five acting nominations for Stephen Graham, Erin Doherty, Owen Cooper, Ashley Walters, and Christine Tremarco. The series, which tells the story of a teenage boy arrested for the murder of a classmate, has captured global attention after its streaming debut in 2025. Other notable nominees include 'A Thousand Blows,' which also features Graham and Doherty, earning seven nominations, and 'Andor,' the Star Wars prequel series, which has six. The BAFTA ceremony will be hosted by Greg Davies, and the winners will be announced on May 10. Other highlights include the BAFTA nod for 'Gaza: Fight For Survival,' Sky News' special program covering the ongoing conflict in Gaza. First-time nominees like Bob Mortimer, Jodie Whittaker, and Paddy Considine showcase a fresh wave of talent in the industry. This year's nominees reflect a diverse range of genres, including drama, comedy, and docume

item.title: Minnesota Files Lawsuit Against Trump Administration Seeking Evidence from Federal Agent Shootings During Immigration Enforcement Operation
item.description: The state of Minnesota, through its Attorney General Keith Ellison, the Hennepin County Attorney’s Office, and the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension, filed a lawsuit in Washington, D.C., against the Trump administration on March 24, 2026. The suit names the Department of Justice, Attorney General Pam Bondi, the Department of Homeland Security, and Secretary Kristi Noem as defendants. It seeks access to evidence related to three incidents during the federal Operation Metro Surge immigration crackdown in the Minneapolis area: the fatal shooting of Renee Good on January 7, 2026, when she reportedly attempted to ram her vehicle into an ICE agent; the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti on January 24, 2026, while he confronted federal agents; and the non-fatal shooting of Julio Sosa-Celis on January 14, 2026, after he allegedly attacked agents with a shovel. Charges against Sosa-Celis were later dismissed amid questions about officers' statements. Minnesota officials argue that federal au

item.title: Safe Tips for Using Red Light Therapy Caps on Color-Treated or Damaged Hair
item.description: Red light therapy caps provide a non-invasive option for supporting hair growth in people with color-treated, bleached, or chemically damaged hair. These devices use specific wavelengths of light to stimulate blood flow and energize hair follicles without harsh chemicals or procedures. For best results and to avoid further damage, users should follow a balanced approach tailored to fragile hair. Experts recommend limiting sessions to three or four times per week, with each lasting 10 to 20 minutes. This schedule allows the scalp adequate rest while promoting consistent improvements in hair density and thickness, often noticeable after four to six months of regular use. Overuse offers no extra benefits and may stress already weakened strands. Preparation is key: always apply the cap to a completely clean and dry scalp. Washing with a mild shampoo removes oils, product buildup, and residues that can block light penetration, which is especially important for color-treated hair that may ha

item.title: The Swasthani Vrata Katha: An Ancient Nepali Hindu Text and Its Complex Impact on Women's Lives
item.description: The Swasthani Vrata Katha, a 16th-century Nepali Hindu devotional text, remains a vibrant part of winter rituals in Nepal. Each year during the cold lunar month of January-February, many Hindu women in red attire participate in the Swasthani vrata, bathing in rivers, eating one meal a day, and focusing on worship of the goddess Swasthani and god Shiva. Families across Nepal recite its 31 chapters nightly, even if only a few observe the full month-long vow. The text blends universal Hindu myths about Shiva with local stories centered on three mortals: young child bride Goma, her dutiful son Navaraj, and his flawed wife Chandravati. Through devotion to Swasthani, they overcome hardship and rise to royal status. Female characters, both mortal and divine like Sati, Parvati, and Vrinda, emphasize unwavering loyalty as wives, often enduring suffering or sacrifice. Scholars and Nepali women interpret the text in two ways. Some see it as reinforcing patriarchal norms prevalent in Nepal, where

item.title: Experts Warn of Potential Dangers from Leaking Radioactive Tomb in the Pacific
item.description: The Runit Dome, a structure built by the United States military on the Marshall Islands in the 1970s, has been leaking radioactive material for decades. Initially created to contain waste from nuclear testing, the dome is now facing rising sea levels and climate change-related threats. The 115-meter-wide concrete dome covers over 120,000 tons of nuclear waste, including dangerous plutonium, and has developed cracks that let water seep through. Despite initial concerns, experts have emphasized that leaks have been small so far, but the situation is worrisome. As sea levels continue to rise, the stability of the dome could be compromised, leading to more significant leaks of dangerous radioactive materials into the Pacific Ocean. This environmental risk is compounded by the ongoing contamination of the surrounding islands and the effects on the local communities who live nearby. Researchers have urged for greater action to address the nuclear legacy left on the islands, including a call

item.title: Former Zutons guitarist Boyan Chowdhury injured in racist assault in Liverpool
item.description: Boyan Chowdhury, a founding member and former guitarist of the Liverpool indie band The Zutons, was seriously injured in a racist attack in the Wavertree area of Liverpool on Saturday afternoon. The incident occurred when Chowdhury asked a group of young males, believed to be in their late teens, to move away while they were smoking near him and his five-year-old son. The group allegedly began shouting racial slurs, including terms targeting his Bangladeshi heritage. After escorting his son to a neighbor's house for safety, Chowdhury was approached from the side and struck on the head with a weapon, believed to be a piece of wood. He instinctively turned and partially blocked the blow, sustaining a deep wound that split his forehead down to the skull. Chowdhury believes that without turning, the blow to the back of his head could have been fatal. He was treated in hospital and has since shared graphic images of his injuries on social media. In an interview with Sky News, the musician d

item.title: A Chicago Family’s Experience Living in Fear of ICE Raids
item.description: Ava and Sam, an undocumented family living in Chicago, have spent months in hiding due to the threat of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raids. Their fear began in the fall when ICE patrols became highly visible in immigrant neighborhoods, creating widespread panic. Incidents of violent arrests and mistaken detentions contributed to a climate of terror. The family’s children experienced trauma from separation and fear, while Ava and Sam struggled with isolation, economic strain, and constant worry about being discovered. Despite receiving Temporary Protected Status for Ava, the family remained cautious as ICE continued operations. The couple adapted their routines to minimize exposure, limited outdoor activities, and relied on social networks and volunteers for necessities. Advanced surveillance technologies, including license-plate readers and mobile tracking, heightened their anxiety. The article highlights the psychological, social, and financial impact of ICE enforcement o

item.title: Personal Reflections on Pluralism and Tolerance from Sri Lanka’s Civil War
item.description: In this reflective piece, a personality psychologist who grew up in Sri Lanka shares how his experiences during the country’s civil war (1983–2009) shaped his understanding of pluralism. Raised in the diverse city of Kandy, surrounded by Buddhist, Hindu, Christian, and Muslim communities, he witnessed both harmonious daily interactions and deep ethnic and religious fractures caused by conflict between the Sinhalese-majority government and Tamil separatists, as well as a separate Marxist insurrection. The author argues that true pluralism goes beyond mere diversity or legal protections—it is a moral virtue requiring active commitment to engage across differences with mutual dignity and respect. He distinguishes pluralism from simple tolerance, describing it as a continuum from hate and indifference to relational engagement, and emphasizes that it involves virtues such as intellectual humility, empathy through perspective-taking, curiosity, self-regulation, and courage. Drawing on person

item.title: Crunchyroll Investigates Data Breach Involving Customer Support Ticket System
item.description: Crunchyroll, the popular anime streaming platform, has confirmed a data breach that occurred after a hacker claimed to have gained unauthorized access to its internal systems and customer data. The incident was linked to a third-party vendor, Telus Digital, which handles customer support for Crunchyroll. Screenshots provided by cybersecurity experts suggest the hacker accessed Crunchyroll’s Zendesk support system, stealing sensitive support ticket information, including over 6.8 million email addresses. The breach reportedly occurred through a compromised Okta single sign-on account belonging to a support agent. Although Crunchyroll has not found any evidence of ongoing unauthorized access, the company is working with cybersecurity professionals to investigate the breach. The stolen data, including support ticket records, appears to have been downloaded until early 2025, when access was revoked. This breach comes after a recent security incident at Telus Digital, although Crunchyroll h

item.title: NASA's Artemis II Mission: Crew to Travel Farther Than Any Human Before
item.description: NASA's Artemis II mission, set to launch as early as April 1, 2026, will mark a historic journey. Four astronauts, including Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen, will travel farther from Earth than any human before. The mission will see the crew fly over the far side of the moon, an area never directly observed by human eyes. The spacecraft will travel an average of 240,000 miles to the moon, with the astronauts reaching up to 250,000 miles at the mission's farthest point. Unlike Apollo missions, which orbited the near side of the moon, Artemis II will fly at a much higher altitude, offering a unique vantage of lunar features. The mission will also test the capabilities of NASA’s Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and the Orion spacecraft. This flight is seen as a critical step for the Artemis program, which aims to eventually establish a crewed lunar station and prepare for Mars exploration. The crew will also conduct lun

item.title: Brad Pitt Reportedly Seeks to End Long-Running Legal Dispute with Angelina Jolie
item.description:
Brad Pitt is reportedly ready to end his lengthy legal dispute with ex-wife Angelina Jolie, signaling a move toward resolution after nearly a decade of conflict. The Hollywood pair, who met during the filming of 'Mr. & Mrs. Smith' in 2003, were married in 2014 and separated two years later, citing irreconcilable differences. They finalized their divorce in 2024 after twelve years together and share six children: Maddox, Pax, Zahara, Shiloh, and twins Knox and Vivienne. In recent years, several of their children have chosen to remove Pitt’s surname from their own, reflecting the ongoing strain within the family.
The couple's legal battle primarily revolves around child custody matters and ownership of their French estate, Château Miraval, a 1,200-acre winery they purchased in 2011. Pitt has claimed that Jolie sold her stake to Russian businessman Yuri Shefler for $67 million without his knowledge, arguing that it was a jointly owned property. This led to Pitt filing a lawsuit in 2022 a

item.title: Glass-Based Quantum Chip Enables High-Speed Secure Communication
item.description: Researchers from the University of Padua, Politecnico di Milano, and the CNR Institute for Photonics and Nanotechnologies have developed a novel quantum communication device using borosilicate glass. Reported in Advanced Photonics, the team created a high-performance, laser-written quantum coherent receiver directly inside the glass. Unlike traditional silicon-based receivers, this glass-based system offers low optical loss, polarization independence, and long-term stability, making it compatible with existing fiber-optic networks. The chip integrates tunable heterodyne detection, beam splitters, thermo-optic phase shifters, and three-dimensional waveguides to enable precise measurement of quantum signals. This versatility allows the same device to function as both a quantum random number generator (QRNG) and a continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) system. The QRNG achieved a record-secure random bit generation rate of 42.7 Gbit/s, while the CV-QKD system delivered a 3.

item.title: How Biometric and Smart Device Data Are Undermining Privacy Rights
item.description: Andrew Guthrie Ferguson’s article explores how the growing use of biometric technologies and smart health devices has eroded Americans’ expectations of privacy. From smartwatches tracking heart rates to DNA databases and facial recognition tools, our biological and behavioral data are increasingly accessible to corporations and law enforcement. What began as a means of promoting health and self-awareness—the so-called 'Internet of Bodies'—has turned into a system of pervasive surveillance. Devices that collect data on exercise, medication, or mental health can now be used in court or investigation contexts, sometimes without users’ consent. Examples include mental health apps like BetterHelp and reproductive health trackers that have shared sensitive information with advertisers or become sources of prosecutorial evidence in abortion-related cases. The FBI’s massive biometric databases, combined with state programs retaining DNA samples—even from newborns—highlight how easily personal

item.title: Engineered air cavities dramatically enhance emission in atom-thin semiconductors
item.description: Researchers have developed a novel approach to significantly boost the optical performance of atomically thin semiconductors without altering the material itself. By placing a single-layer tungsten disulfide (WS2) over nanoscale air cavities, called Mie voids, etched into bismuth telluride (Bi2Te3), they created highly efficient light traps. Unlike traditional dielectric resonators that confine light within solid materials, Mie voids trap light in subwavelength air cavities, concentrating the optical field exactly where the WS2 layer sits. This 'inverted' confinement leads to up to 20 times stronger photoluminescence and 25 times enhanced nonlinear optical signals. The cavities were precisely designed through electromagnetic simulations and fabricated using focused ion beam milling, allowing for tunable resonances that align with WS2 exciton emission. Measurements confirmed that the enhanced emission is due to the engineered cavity modes rather than material variations, and the setup a

item.title: S&P Global PMI Shows Sharpest Rise in UK Manufacturing Input Costs Since 1992 Amid Iran War Disruptions
item.description: A flash S&P Global Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) survey released on March 24, 2026, reveals that British businesses experienced the slowest pace of activity growth in six months during March due to the ongoing US-Israeli war with Iran. The composite PMI, covering manufacturing and non-retail services, fell to 51.0 from 53.7 in February, remaining in expansion territory but signaling clear economic strain. Manufacturers faced the most dramatic impact, with input costs surging at the fastest monthly rate since the 1992 sterling crisis following Black Wednesday. This sharp acceleration was driven primarily by soaring prices for fuel, transportation, and energy-intensive raw materials, as Brent crude oil rose nearly 50% and natural gas prices jumped over 90% since hostilities began on February 28. Businesses responded by raising their own prices at the quickest pace since April 2025, while employment in the sector declined for the 18th consecutive month. The report highlights how the co

item.title: Reflecting on César Chávez's Legacy Amid New Allegations
item.description: In the wake of new sexual assault allegations against César Chávez, many are reevaluating his legacy and removing his name and image from public spaces. Despite this, columnist Gustavo Arellano decides to keep a framed photograph of Chávez and his co-founder Dolores Huerta in his office. Arellano reflects on how Chávez's leadership in the farmworkers' movement should not be forgotten, even as new allegations complicate his legacy. He notes the complexity of honoring historical figures who have both done great good and committed serious harm. The article discusses the swift removal of Chávez’s presence from public life in California, and Arellano argues that while communities have the right to make their own decisions about honoring Chávez, erasing him too quickly oversimplifies the narrative. His decision to keep the photograph serves as a reminder of both the good Chávez accomplished and the painful truths about his actions. Arellano emphasizes that we should not blindly elevate figur

item.title: Stellar Rotation Enhances Mixing in Red Giant Stars, Solving Longstanding Surface Chemistry Mystery
item.description: Astronomers have resolved a decades-old puzzle about red giant stars: how material from their nuclear-burning cores reaches the surface, altering the star's chemical composition. For nearly 50 years, scientists observed changes in surface abundances, such as shifts in the carbon-12 to carbon-13 ratio, during the red giant branch phase, but a stable radiative barrier layer inside the star seemed to prevent mixing. Using advanced 3D hydrodynamical simulations on powerful supercomputers like Canada's new Trillium cluster and resources at the Texas Advanced Computing Center, researchers from the University of Victoria and the University of Minnesota demonstrated that stellar rotation dramatically amplifies the effectiveness of internal gravity waves generated by the convective envelope. These waves, previously thought to transport minimal material, can boost mixing rates by more than 100 times when the star is rotating, fully accounting for the observed chemical signatures in typical red g

item.title: NASA Releases High-Resolution Images Revealing Detailed Views of Solar System Planets
item.description:
NASA has shared a stunning collection of high-resolution images that provide some of the clearest and most detailed views yet of the planets in our solar system. What were once seen as mere distant points of light in the night sky are now revealed in extraordinary clarity, showcasing the unique characteristics of each world. The imagery highlights the planets in their natural order from the Sun outward.
Mercury appears as a small, cratered, airless world resembling our Moon, with extreme temperature swings and evidence of a violent past marked by impact basins and lava plains. Venus, similar in size to Earth, is shrouded in thick clouds of carbon dioxide and sulfuric acid, creating a runaway greenhouse effect that makes it the hottest planet in the system. Earth stands out as the only known planet supporting life, with vast oceans and a balanced atmosphere.
Mars is depicted as a cold, red desert world covered in iron-rich dust, featuring massive geological formations like Olympus Mon

item.title: Researchers Develop Peptide-Based Oral Insulin Delivery System
item.description: For over a century, scientists have attempted to develop insulin pills to replace daily injections for diabetes management, but the digestive system has historically destroyed the insulin before it could be absorbed. Researchers at Kumamoto University, led by Associate Professor Shingo Ito, have developed a new method using a small cyclic peptide called DNP that allows insulin to pass through the small intestine. They tested two approaches: one where insulin is mixed with a modified peptide and stabilized with zinc, and another where the peptide is covalently attached to insulin using click chemistry. Both methods successfully lowered blood sugar levels in diabetic mouse models and maintained stable glucose control with daily dosing. Importantly, this approach significantly reduces the amount of insulin needed for oral delivery, achieving 33-41% bioavailability compared to injections. The team believes this technology could eventually enable oral insulin for human patients and may also

item.title: Olivia Rodrigo's Concert Donation Leads to Life-Saving Cancer Screening for Fan’s Aunt
item.description: A fan shared a heartwarming story online about how Olivia Rodrigo's charity concert in the Philippines led to a life-saving outcome for her aunt. The singer's concert, where ticket proceeds were donated to Jhpiego, a nonprofit focused on women's health, funded free cervical cancer screenings. The fan explained that her aunt, who attended the concert, learned about Jhpiego through the event and received a screening that detected cancer early enough to save her life. The fan also mentioned that two days after the concert, there was an HPV vaccination drive at her niece's school. This incident highlights the wider impact of celebrity-driven charity efforts, particularly in raising awareness for preventative healthcare such as early cancer screenings. Fans have praised Rodrigo's initiative for using her platform to support social causes, though the singer has not commented publicly on the specific case. Early detection of cervical cancer is crucial to improving survival rates, making initi

item.title: UK Chancellor Announces Measures to Address Iran War’s Impact on Energy Prices
item.description:
UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves will deliver a statement in Parliament outlining measures to combat rising prices caused by the ongoing conflict in Iran. The war has severely disrupted global energy markets, pushing oil and gas prices higher due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial supply route through which 20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas normally pass. Reeves is expected to unveil an 'anti-profiteering framework' empowering regulators, such as the Competition and Markets Authority, to identify and take action against companies exploiting the crisis. This framework could include temporary powers to curb unfair price increases in sectors like fuel and groceries.
Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer has confirmed that the government is also considering extending energy bill support for households through the end of the year, although not at the same scale as in 2022 due to tighter public finances. In addition, Reeves will emphasize the importance of diversifying B

item.title: Miley Cyrus jokes about Alex Cooper being 'creepy' after becoming neighbors
item.description: During a recent appearance on the 'Call Her Daddy' podcast, Miley Cyrus jokingly called podcast host Alex Cooper 'creepy,' after recalling an unexpected coincidence where Cooper became her neighbor. The story took fans by surprise as Cyrus shared how Cooper, a huge fan of hers, once interviewed her and expressed her admiration. Things took a bizarre turn when Cooper later realized where Cyrus lived and ended up moving into a house right next door. Cyrus recalled how Cooper reacted with disbelief, stating, 'Oh my god, you live here?' The interaction felt like a surreal full circle moment for Cyrus, as Cooper had previously interviewed her at her home. Despite the lighthearted 'creepy' comment, Cyrus emphasized that she was joking and praised Cooper for her dedication to the podcast and for being an enthusiastic supporter of Miley's career, especially her time on 'Hannah Montana.' The playful exchange quickly went viral, with fans enjoying the humorous and strange twist of events. Cyrus'

item.title: Donna Miller Wins Illinois Democratic Primary for 2nd Congressional District
item.description: Cook County Commissioner Donna Miller has won the Democratic primary for Illinois’ 2nd Congressional District, defeating former Rep. Jesse Jackson Jr. and several other candidates. Jackson, who resigned in 2012 following a federal corruption investigation and served prison time for misusing campaign funds, attempted a political comeback leveraging his name recognition. Despite national attention and significant support from the Leading the Future PAC, backed by OpenAI stakeholders, Jackson was unable to overcome the public perception of his past misconduct. Miller, by contrast, consolidated support across key areas of the district and benefited from more than $4 million in ad spending by a group aligned with the American Israel Public Affairs Committee. Her campaign emphasized public health, public safety, and fiscal oversight, while highlighting her leadership roles in Democratic women’s organizations and Planned Parenthood of Illinois. She focused on promoting her record rather than

item.title: Colombian Military Plane Crash Near Puerto Leguízamo Leaves at Least 64 Dead
item.description: At least 64 people lost their lives after a Colombian military C-130 Hercules aircraft crashed shortly after takeoff from Caucaya Airport in southern Colombia, near Puerto Leguízamo, Putumayo. The plane, carrying 114 passengers, including 112 soldiers, two police officers, and 11 crew members, broke apart and caught fire upon impact, according to regional Governor Jhon Gabriel Molina. Despite the presence of survivors—some treated locally and others transported to Bogotá for advanced medical care—the tragedy marks one of the deadliest aviation incidents in Colombia in recent years. Defense Minister Pedro Sánchez stated that the aircraft was deemed airworthy and operated by qualified personnel, ruling out any immediate indication of an attack. Preliminary footage verified by CNN shows the aircraft ascending before losing altitude and crashing less than two miles from the runway. President Gustavo Petro expressed deep sorrow, criticizing delays in modernizing the armed forces and citing

item.title: How Strategic Oil Reserves Are Being Used to Stabilize Prices Amid the Iran War
item.description: In response to the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has cut off about 20% of the world's oil supply, the International Energy Agency announced the largest coordinated release of strategic oil reserves in history. Starting in late March 2026, 32 countries will release a total of 412 million barrels over four months to mitigate supply disruptions and stabilize global oil prices. The United States is contributing 172 million barrels, nearly half of the total, which will temporarily reduce its Strategic Petroleum Reserve to its lowest level since the early 1980s. Strategic reserves were first created to secure emergency oil supplies, dating back to the U.S. Navy’s early 20th-century fuel stockpiles. Today, reserves serve to buffer price spikes and provide emergency oil during crises. While the release provides several million barrels per day, it cannot fully replace the 10 million barrels per day disrupted by the Strait of Hormuz closure. The move is intended to prev

item.title: Trump delays Iran ultimatum amid missile strikes and market reactions
item.description: Tensions in the Middle East escalated as Iranian missile attacks targeted multiple cities in Israel, including Tel Aviv and Nesher, causing damage to buildings, vehicles, and wounding civilians. Israeli forces responded with strikes on Iranian and Lebanese targets, continuing the regional conflict. US President Donald Trump announced a five-day postponement of his ultimatum for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, claiming progress on talks with Tehran. Iran, however, denied any dialogue with the US, dismissing Trump’s statement as a tactic to lower energy prices and gain time for military planning. The conflict has disrupted global oil markets, prompting temporary measures in Asia, including Japan releasing national oil reserves, South Korea implementing energy-saving campaigns, and China limiting fuel price hikes to stabilize its economy. The violence has also exacerbated the humanitarian situation, with casualties reported in Lebanon and ongoing threats to civilians in Israel and Ir

item.title: New Jersey Morgue Employee Terminated for Sharing Confidential Information About Gaudreau Brothers’ Deaths
item.description: A New Jersey Civil Service Commission has upheld the firing of a morgue worker who leaked confidential details regarding the deaths of NHL players Johnny Gaudreau and his brother Matthew Gaudreau, who both died in a bicycle accident in 2024. The employee, who was only 14 at the time of the incident, shared information and notes about the brothers’ deaths with friends via a private group chat, violating a nondisclosure agreement he had signed. One recipient later posted the information online, causing it to spread publicly and prompting media inquiries to the New Jersey State Police. The employee admitted to acting out of emotion and shock after the tragic accident. The brothers were struck by a vehicle in Oldmans Township just one day before their sister’s wedding. Sean Higgins, the driver involved in the crash, faces multiple serious charges including first-degree aggravated manslaughter and reckless vehicular homicide, although he denies responsibility and disputes the blood alcohol

item.title: Oil climbs back above $100 per barrel amid ongoing Middle East conflict
item.description: Oil prices moved back above $100 per barrel late on Monday after an earlier dip in trading, reflecting renewed market anxiety over the ongoing war between Iran and a U.S.–Israel alliance. Brent crude, the global benchmark, reached about $103.71 a barrel after falling earlier in the session to around $97, as investors reacted to shifting messaging from President Trump about potential strikes on Iranian energy targets and the status of talks with Tehran. Reports indicated that Iran denied any negotiations with the United States, even as missiles or drones reportedly struck at oil‑rich Gulf states such as Israel, Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, reinforcing fears of supply disruptions. The renewed surge in oil underscores how the Middle East conflict is feeding into global energy markets, with traders closely watching both military developments and diplomatic signals. Higher prices threaten to push up gasoline and diesel costs for consumers and to weigh on already fragile financial markets, espec

item.title: Vibration-Activated Tooth Powder Whitens and Protects Teeth Safely
item.description: Researchers have developed an innovative tooth powder that whitens teeth safely while repairing enamel and promoting oral health. Unlike traditional peroxide-based whitening products that can weaken enamel, this experimental powder, called BSCT, is activated by the vibrations of an electric toothbrush. The powder is made by combining strontium and calcium ions with barium titanate, forming a ceramic that generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) only when in motion, targeting stains without causing harm. Laboratory tests on human teeth stained with tea and coffee showed that after four hours of brushing, teeth were visibly whiter, and after 12 hours, they were nearly 50% brighter than teeth brushed with a saline solution. Additionally, the powder promoted enamel and dentin repair through mineral deposition. Animal studies in rats on high-sugar diets showed that daily brushing with the powder rebalanced the oral microbiome, reducing harmful bacteria such as Porphyromonas gingivalis and St

item.title: James Carville Rebukes Bill Maher Over Trump Criticism on Podcast
item.description: Democratic strategist James Carville unleashed a profanity-laced response to comedian Bill Maher on the 'Politics War Room' podcast during a Politicon event. Carville directly addressed Maher's accusation that he suffers from 'Trump Derangement Syndrome,' calling him a 'crazy old man' disconnected from everyday Americans. 'Do me a favor, Bill. Try to get your head out of Bari Weiss’s a--,' Carville retorted, dismissing Maher's view that critics like him are driven by blind rage. He embraced the 'crazy' label, admitting he wakes up furious at 2 a.m., unable to believe Donald Trump is president again, stating, 'I can’t believe this motherf----- is the President of the United States.' Carville argued that his intense reaction proves sanity amid the political climate, especially citing Trump's response to Robert Mueller's death where the president said he was 'glad.' He referenced Trump's past comments and recent events, insisting critics won't back down despite mockery. The exchange highl

item.title: Analysis of US Military Struggles Against Iran
item.description: The article discusses the ongoing military tensions between the United States and Iran, highlighting significant challenges faced by US forces. According to the report, Iran has successfully destroyed ten advanced US radar systems, crucial for detecting and intercepting Iranian missiles and drones. Key US military bases, including those protecting the US embassy in Baghdad, lost radar coverage, forcing the US to import high-tech radar systems from South Korea, which has caused diplomatic friction. The piece argues that without sufficient radar, US bases, Gulf oil facilities, naval vessels, and Israeli targets are vulnerable to thousands of Iranian missiles and drones. Observers cited in the article suggest the US may exhaust its missile interceptors and offensive capabilities before Iran depletes its own arsenal. The report also criticizes former President Trump’s initial strategy to quickly overthrow the Iranian regime, stating that it failed and inadvertently strengthened domestic Ir

item.title: Centrist former Representative Melissa Bean wins Illinois’ 8th District Democratic primary
item.description:
Former U.S. Representative Melissa Bean has won the Democratic primary for Illinois’ 8th Congressional District, marking a political comeback after losing her seat in 2010 during the Tea Party wave. Bean, a moderate Democrat, ran on a message of pragmatism and experience, arguing that voters in the Chicago‑area district want functional, less partisan governance. She defeated several more progressive challengers, including tech entrepreneur Junaid Ahmed, in a race that drew significant national attention.
The contest highlighted divisions within the Democratic Party over foreign‑policy positions, particularly regarding Israel, and became a proxy battle between establishment and progressive wings. Bean benefited from heavy financial support from outside groups linked to the American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) and other pro‑Israel donors, as well as from tech and AI‑backed money streams. In contrast, progressive groups such as Justice Democrats spent relatively modest sums a

item.title: Escalation and Diplomacy in the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict of 2026
item.description:
The Wall Street Journal’s live coverage tracks an intensifying confrontation between the United States, Israel, and Iran in early 2026, marked by cross‑border strikes, missile attacks, and failed or ambiguous diplomatic overtures. President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. would delay further attacks on Iranian power facilities and energy infrastructure for five days, citing constructive back‑channel talks with Tehran led by top envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner. Trump suggested that any agreement would demand a ban on Iranian nuclear weapons and a broader shift in the regime’s behavior, while insisting that Iran was eager to resolve the conflict.
Iran’s foreign‑ministry officials, however, publicly denied holding formal negotiations with Washington, saying only that regional mediators were promoting dialogue. At the same time, missile and drone attacks from Iran continued, targeting Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and other Gulf states, while Israel mounted new strikes on Iranian site

item.title: Pentagon Official Emil Michael Reflects on Uber Exit and Ongoing Dispute with Anthropic
item.description:
Emil Michael, currently serving as a senior technology official at the U.S. Department of Defense, has resurfaced in the headlines amid the Pentagon’s clash with AI company Anthropic. In a recent podcast interview with Kleiner Perkins partner Joubin Mirzadegan, Michael discussed both the government’s AI policy challenges and his past at Uber. The conversation, recorded before the Defense Department’s conflict with Anthropic intensified, also revealed long-held resentment about his and Travis Kalanick’s 2017 departures from Uber. Michael admitted he remains deeply bitter about being forced out during the fallout from investigations into Uber’s workplace culture, even though he wasn’t personally implicated. He blames major investors, including Benchmark, for prioritizing short-term gains over Uber’s long-term vision of autonomous driving. The pair’s ouster, he claimed, derailed Uber’s leadership in self-driving technology, a setback he believes continues to haunt the company.
Michael al

item.title: Los Angeles Jury Orders Bill Cosby to Pay $59.25 Million in 1972 Sexual Assault Civil Case
item.description:
A civil jury in Los Angeles has found comedian and actor Bill Cosby liable for a 1972 sexual assault against Donna Motsinger, now 84 years old. The jury awarded Motsinger a total of $59.25 million in damages, including $19.25 million in compensatory damages and $40 million in punitive damages. Motsinger filed the lawsuit in 2023, alleging that Cosby drugged and raped her after inviting her to a show and providing her with wine that left her incapacitated. According to court filings, she recalled losing consciousness and later waking up partially undressed, concluding she had been assaulted.
The lawsuit also accused Cosby of engaging in a pattern of sexual misconduct involving multiple women over several decades, leveraging his fame and influence. Cosby did not testify during the trial. He has consistently denied all allegations of sexual assault, despite being accused by at least 60 women over the years.
Cosby was previously convicted of sexual assault in 2018 in a separate case, but

item.title: Understanding the Psychological Roots of Silence in Alleged Abuse by Cesar Chavez
item.description:
The Conversation article explores why multiple women who accused Mexican American civil rights leader Cesar Chavez of sexual abuse waited decades before speaking publicly. The allegations, uncovered by The New York Times after a yearslong investigation, reveal patterns of silence consistent with betrayal trauma — a psychological response that occurs when victims are harmed by people they deeply trust or depend on. The piece explains that victims in such scenarios often suppress or dissociate from their experiences to preserve essential relationships, financial support, or community belonging. These dynamics are intensified when the abuser holds social, cultural, or institutional power.
Clinical psychologist research cited in the article connects this silence to 'betrayal trauma theory,' which shows that disclosure often carries heavy emotional, social, and practical risks. Victims may fear disbelief, blame, or damaging the reputations of institutions and causes they value — a form of

item.title: Seedance 2.0 AI Shows Potential in Film Creation, But Challenges Remain
item.description: The rise of AI-generated video is starting to impact multiple industries, and the film sector is particularly concerned. While most AI clips on social media are superficial or humorous, deepfakes and educational videos for children are raising ethical and misinformation concerns. The latest development, Seedance 2.0, created by TikTok's parent company ByteDance, represents a significant step forward in AI video generation. Trained on billions of posts, it can produce content that balances entertainment with educational elements, as demonstrated in examples like 'Chloe vs History,' where a digital influencer can travel through time. Despite its capabilities, Seedance 2.0 is not yet capable of independently creating truly watchable films, though it does present a disruptive tool for creators. US studios are wary of AI profiting from their copyrighted material and potentially affecting the industry's economic model. The technology is both promising and controversial, highlighting the tens

item.title: Postmenopausal Women Lose Significantly More Weight When Combining Hormone Therapy with Tirzepatide
item.description: A recent Mayo Clinic study has found that postmenopausal women who combined menopausal hormone therapy with tirzepatide, a medication approved for overweight and obesity, lost approximately 35% more weight than those taking the drug alone. The study analyzed 120 adults treated with tirzepatide for at least 12 months, comparing outcomes between those using hormone therapy and those who were not. While the study was observational and not randomized, suggesting that other factors like lifestyle or improved sleep may contribute, researchers noted the results indicate a potential synergy between estrogen and GLP-1-based medications. Estrogen may enhance the appetite-suppressing effects of drugs like tirzepatide, opening new possibilities for more effective and personalized approaches to weight management in postmenopausal women. Future research will focus on confirming these results through randomized clinical trials and exploring additional cardiometabolic benefits. This work highlights th

item.title: Researchers Identify Protein Interaction Driving Alzheimer’s and Develop Compound to Halt It in Mice
item.description: A research team led by Professor Hilmar Bading at Heidelberg University has uncovered a key biological mechanism behind Alzheimer's disease progression. The scientists identified a harmful interaction between two proteins—the NMDA receptor and the TRPM4 ion channel—that, when bound together, form a destructive protein complex. This complex triggers neuronal death and accelerates memory loss. Using a specially developed compound called FP802, the researchers successfully disrupted this toxic interaction in a mouse model of Alzheimer’s. The drug, described as a TwinF Interface Inhibitor, binds to the exact site where the two proteins connect, preventing the formation of the damaging complex. Mice treated with FP802 showed slower disease progression, preserved memory and learning abilities, and reduced brain damage. The treatment also lowered the accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques, a hallmark of Alzheimer’s pathology. Unlike traditional therapies focused solely on amyloid removal, this

item.title: Swish secures $38 million Series B to expand its fast food delivery model in India
item.description:
Bengaluru-based food delivery startup Swish has raised $38 million in a Series B round led by Hara Global and Bain Capital Ventures, with participation from Accel, Stride Ventures, and Alteria Capital. This brings Swish’s total funding to $54 million and values the company at $139 million post-money, more than double its valuation from a year ago. Founded in 2024, Swish operates a 10-minute fresh food delivery service targeting dense urban areas of Bengaluru. The startup owns its entire operation—kitchens, supply chain, and delivery fleet—allowing for tighter cost control and faster order fulfillment compared to aggregators like Swiggy or Zomato that rely on partner restaurants.
Swish claims to deliver around 20,000 orders per day, up from 5,000 just four months ago, across ten micro-markets. It offers over 200 dishes and beverages, with an average ticket size of ₹200–₹250 (approximately $2–$3). CEO and co-founder Aniket Shah described the business model as a hybrid between restauran

item.title: Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss Secures Illinois 9th District Democratic Nomination
item.description: Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss has won the Democratic primary for Illinois' 9th congressional district, succeeding longtime Rep. Jan Schakowsky after her retirement announcement. The Chicago-area seat, held by Democrats for decades and home to a significant Jewish population, drew national attention due to heavy involvement from pro-Israel groups like AIPAC, which spent over $5 million on ads boosting rival Laura Fine and later attacking Biss and progressive Kat Abughazaleh. Biss, a former state legislator and University of Chicago math professor who ran unsuccessfully for governor in 2018, overcame the crowded field of over a dozen candidates with endorsements from Schakowsky, Sen. Elizabeth Warren, and J Street, a liberal pro-Israel group. He criticized AIPAC's interference in Democratic primaries while aligning with Schakowsky's views on Israel and progressive priorities. His platform emphasized affordable housing investment, Social Security expansion, and banning congressional stock tr

item.title: Typical Lifespan of Rental Property Carpets and Responsibility for Replacement
item.description:
This article explains how long carpets typically last in rental properties and clarifies when landlords or tenants are responsible for replacement. In general, carpets in rentals last between 5 and 7 years, although lower-quality carpets may wear out in as little as 3 to 5 years, while higher-end or commercial-grade options can last up to 10 years with proper care. The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development considers five years to be the standard useful life for rental carpets, which is important when determining depreciation and whether landlords can charge tenants for damage.
Landlords are usually responsible for replacing carpets due to normal wear and tear, such as fading, thinning fibers, or minor marks from everyday use. These conditions are expected over time and cannot be charged to tenants. However, landlords must also ensure the property remains safe and habitable, meaning carpets must be replaced if they become hazardous or develop issues like mold.
Tenants may b

item.title: Air Street Capital Raises $232 Million Fund to Back Early-Stage AI Startups
item.description:
London-based Air Street Capital has announced the launch of its third fund, raising $232 million to invest in early-stage artificial intelligence startups across Europe and North America. The firm, led by Nathan Benaich, is positioning itself as one of the largest solo general partner venture capital firms in Europe with this latest raise. The new fund will primarily target early-stage companies, with initial check sizes ranging from $500,000 to $15 million, while select growth-stage investments may reach up to $25 million.
Air Street has built a strong reputation in the AI investment space, having previously backed notable companies such as Black Forest Labs and ElevenLabs, both considered leading AI unicorns. The firm has also achieved successful exits, including Adept, which was acquired by Amazon, and Graphcore, which was sold to SoftBank. These outcomes highlight the firm’s ability to identify and support high-impact AI ventures.
With the addition of Fund III, Air Street now man

item.title: A Cold War‑Style Numbers Station Resurfaces Over Iran During US–Israel Strikes
item.description: At the start of the US–Israel military campaign against Iran on February 28, 2026, a shortwave radio transmission began airing twice daily on the 7910 kHz frequency, later switching to 7842 kHz after jamming attempts. The broadcast features a man’s voice in Persian repeatedly saying 'Tavajoh!' ('Attention!') before reciting long strings of seemingly random numbers, consistent with a Cold War‑era intelligence technique known as a numbers station. Using radio‑location methods, the monitoring group Priyom has traced the signal to a US military shortwave facility within Böblingen Air Base in southwest Germany, likely linked to the US Army’s 52nd Strategic Signal Battalion. Despite that partial identification, the true operator and intended recipient of the broadcast remain unknown. Security experts and former intelligence officers describe numbers stations as encrypted shortwave channels historically used by spy agencies to send covert instructions to agents, relying on codebooks to decode

item.title: 'The View' Co-Host Sara Haines Supports ICE Assistance at Airports During TSA Challenges
item.description: During a segment on 'The View,' co-host Sara Haines addressed the idea of deploying U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents to assist airport security amid the ongoing Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown. Haines expressed support for the measure, highlighting that TSA agents have been forced to work without pay, causing extensive travel delays. She argued that ICE agents, with extensive federal experience and a large budget, could provide needed manpower at airports, ensuring passenger safety. Guest co-host Abby Huntsman emphasized the public concern over airport security during the shutdown, noting that millions of travelers are affected. Co-host Sunny Hostin disagreed, pointing out that ICE agents receive significantly less training than TSA personnel and are trained for different operational tasks, making them less suitable for airport security. Haines countered that the risk in airports is lower than in ICE operations and emphasized that pragmatic solutions

item.title: Startup R3 Bio Develops Brainless Organ Systems as Ethical Alternative to Animal Testing and Future Human Organ Source
item.description: A Bay Area biotech startup called R3 Bio is developing genetically engineered 'organ sacks'—whole organ systems lacking a brain to eliminate sentience and pain perception—as a replacement for animal testing in drug development. Cofounder Alice Gilman explains that these structures would include typical organs but exclude brain development through stem cell technology and gene editing, starting with mouse models and aiming for monkey versions to test drug toxicity more scalably and ethically. The approach addresses shortages of research monkeys in the US, exacerbated by export bans from China, federal phase-outs of animal experimentation under the Trump administration, and ethical concerns from animal rights groups. R3 Bio draws from the 'three Rs' philosophy (replacement, reduction, refinement) in animal research. Beyond testing, the long-term vision includes creating nonsentient human 'organ sacks' or 'bodyoids' to supply tissues, blood, and organs for transplant patients, tackling th

item.title: Leaked DarkSword Exploit Puts Millions of Older iPhones at Risk
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered that a new version of the iPhone hacking tool DarkSword has been publicly leaked on GitHub. This exploit kit targets iPhones and iPads running older versions of iOS, including iOS 18, making hundreds of millions of devices vulnerable. Experts warn that the leaked files are simple HTML and JavaScript scripts, which means even individuals without iOS security knowledge can deploy them quickly to steal sensitive data. The malware can exfiltrate contacts, messages, call history, and passwords stored in iOS keychain to attacker-controlled servers. Researchers from iVerify and Google confirm that the exploits work out of the box and share infrastructure with previous DarkSword versions. Apple has responded by issuing emergency updates for devices unable to run the latest iOS 26 and recommends keeping software up to date, along with using Lockdown Mode to block these attacks. The leak follows the recent discovery of another advanced iPhone hacking too

item.title: Study Reveals Public Misperceptions of Foods' Environmental Impacts
item.description: A new study from the University of Nottingham's School of Psychology shows that people frequently misjudge the environmental impact of everyday foods. Researchers had 168 UK participants sort a wide range of supermarket items into categories based on perceived environmental harm. Many assumed processed foods and animal products were the worst offenders, but they often overlooked the high impact of water-intensive items like nuts and underestimated beef's outsized footprint compared to chicken or other meats. These errors stem from simplistic heuristics, such as animal vs. plant distinctions or processing levels, rather than comprehensive life cycle assessments that factor in emissions, land use, water consumption, and waste from production to disposal. The research, funded by UKRI's Smart Data Research UK and published in the Journal of Cleaner Production, highlights food production's role in greenhouse gas emissions and biodiversity loss. Participants were surprised by scientific impa

item.title: Jessie J Hospitalized for MRI After Hitting Head on Car Roof During China Tour, Rules Out Neck Fracture
item.description: British singer Jessie J, known for hits like 'Price Tag,' experienced a health scare while on her No Secrets tour in China. In a video posted to Instagram, the 37-year-old artist was seen struggling to get into the back of an SUV, where she hit her head hard on the roof. She later shared that she was rushed to the hospital for an MRI scan due to concerns about a possible broken neck. Jessie J admitted she initially thought she had fractured her neck but was relieved to learn it was not broken, though she sustained injuries to her neck and back. Despite the pain and limited head movement, she pushed through with painkillers and continued tour rehearsals and performances. This incident follows a challenging period for the singer, who was diagnosed with early-stage breast cancer in June 2025. She underwent a double mastectomy and was briefly hospitalized again weeks later for a suspected blood clot in her lungs. Jessie J has been open about her recovery journey, including moments of fear

item.title: Jet Fuel Prices Double Amid Iran Conflict, Prompting U.S. and Global Airlines to Cut Flights and Raise Fares
item.description: Jet fuel prices in the United States have more than doubled in recent weeks, rising from approximately $2.17 per gallon to $4.56 per gallon by March 20, 2026, according to the Argus U.S. Jet Fuel Index. This sharp increase is driven by escalating tensions in the Middle East, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint that handles about 20 million barrels of oil per day and significant volumes of jet fuel. The Middle East supplies roughly 17% of global jet fuel consumption, making the market highly sensitive to disruptions. Major U.S. airlines are already feeling the impact. United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby announced plans to reduce about 5% of scheduled flights in the near term and suspend select international routes, including those to Israel and Dubai. The carrier warned that sustained high prices could add $11 billion in annual expenses. Delta Air Lines CEO Ed Bastian reported an additional $400 million in fuel costs for March alone, while American Airlines proje

item.title: Study Shows Forests Accumulate Microplastics Primarily Through Atmospheric Deposition
item.description: A recent study by geoscientists at TU Darmstadt, published in Communications Earth & Environment, reveals that forests are accumulating significant amounts of microplastics, primarily through airborne pathways rather than local ground sources. Researchers collected and analyzed samples from soil, fallen leaves, and atmospheric deposition at four forest sites east of Darmstadt, Germany, using advanced spectroscopic techniques. The findings indicate that tiny plastic particles settle on tree canopies—a process called the 'comb-out effect'—before being transferred to the forest floor via rainfall or autumn leaf fall in deciduous forests. Once on the ground, natural decomposition of leaf litter and biological activity, such as soil organisms, help incorporate these particles deeper into the soil layers, turning forests into long-term reservoirs for this pollution. The highest concentrations appear in the upper leaf litter, but notable amounts reach mineral soil horizons. Modeling suggests

item.title: Russia restricts access to Archive.today, a website for bypassing paywalls
item.description: Russian authorities have blocked the popular paywall bypass website Archive.today, along with its associated domains including .is and .ph. When accessed from Russia, visitors encounter a message stating that the resource is blocked by government decision, citing the federal censorship agency Roskomnadzor. While the official reason for the restriction has not been disclosed, Roskomnadzor confirmed that access to Archive.is is limited but provided no further explanation. Despite the block, the site remains accessible from other regions and users can still archive web pages. Archive.today allows users to save and view copies of websites, including content behind subscription or paywall restrictions. The website recently faced controversy when Wikipedia editors removed links to it, citing a discovery that Archive.today’s code directed visitors’ browsers to send automated traffic to a blogger critical of the service. There is currently no comment from Archive.today regarding the Russian bl

item.title: How Workplace Expectations of Health Deepen the Struggle for Employees Living with Chronic Pain
item.description: Nearly one in four adults in the United States lives with chronic pain, a growing issue that affects not only personal well-being but also the economy, with an estimated loss of $722 billion in productivity each year. A new study published in the Academy of Management Journal examined how professional expectations shape the daily experiences of workers living with long-term pain. Researchers interviewed 66 U.S. employees across various professions—including teachers, lawyers, police officers, and grocery store workers—who described feeling pressured to maintain an image of the 'ideal worker body'—healthy, active, and tireless. To avoid judgment and keep their jobs, many masked their pain, avoided visible coping measures, and pushed their bodies beyond safe limits. This led to worsening symptoms, diminished productivity, and in many cases, job loss or disability. However, some participants found healthier paths forward by seeking supportive employers and doctors who validated their cond

item.title: EY-Parthenon Estimates 40% U.S. Recession Risk with Potential for Quick Increase
item.description: In a Wall Street Journal live market coverage update dated March 23, 2026, EY-Parthenon, the strategy consulting arm of Ernst & Young, has raised its assessment of U.S. recession odds to 40% for the coming period.
